summary.what: Phase 1 tally for v0.23.0 cross-agent debt classification
summary.why: Plan doc called for a tally before locking heuristic defaults. User pointed at ~/Projects/constructured/osprey-strike as the source of best signal (multi-author, bot-heavy, real-world multi-agent workflows — origin of most recent timbers feature work). Without the tally, the heuristic defaults were guesses; with it, they're grounded in real usage patterns from the repo whose pain motivated the reframe.
summary.how: Ran git log --first-parent --no-merges with %aE (mailmap-resolved author email) + %ct (CommitDate) against 100-commit windows in osprey-strike and 50 in timbers. Wrote awk simulation of the heuristic for several 'now' anchors. Discovered osprey-strike's existing .mailmap coalesces Bob's two emails (bob@redshifted.io ↔ robert.bergman@gmail.com) — a 61% silent false-negative rate on Bob's own work if timbers used %ae instead of %aE. Mailmap is git's canonical mechanism for the multi-email case and gets us the right answer for free. Inlined the tally as a 77-line addendum to the plan doc; updated the Heuristics section to mandate %aE and the deferred-directives section to kill session-author entirely (mailmap supersedes).

notes: Tally confirmed every earlier review decision (24h window, OR composition, strict-mailmap-equality, CommitDate over AuthorDate) plus surfaced ONE new load-bearing requirement (%aE not %ae). Tally was 30-min of shell + analysis, well within the 'shrunk to ~10 min' frame the pragmatist proposed — slightly over because I ran two repos and three different 'now' anchors instead of one. Worth it: osprey-strike's mailmap is the single most important plan-changing finding from the entire review + tally cycle. Phase 2 (extend git.Commit struct with CommitDate + switch %ae→%aE) is now unblocked.
